raw_data:
b' Money Stuff
Simplifying a bit, the way a special purpose acquisition company works is that the sponsor of the SPAC raises a pot of money by selling shar

SPAC SPAC SPAC

Simplifying a bit, the way a special purpose acquisition company works is that the sponsor of the SPAC raises a pot of money by selling shares to public investors at $10 per share. Say the sponsor offers 20 million shares, so the pot of money has $200 million in it. Then she goes out and looks for a private target company to merge with to take it public. Say she finds a private company that is worth $800 million. (The valuation of a private company will always be uncertain, but let\xe2\x80\x99s assume for now that the valuation of this company is certain, and that it\xe2\x80\x99s $800 million.) If the target company is worth $800 million, and the SPAC is just a pot of money with $200 million in it, then the combined company should be worth $1 billion. So the sponsor and the target company negotiate a merger in which the existing shareholders of the target company get 80% of the shares of the combined company ($800 million worth) and the SPAC shareholders get 20% ($200 million worth). (The merger\xc2\xa0is sometimes called a \xe2\x80\x9cde-SPAC merger.\xe2\x80\x9d)\xc2\xa0The public investors in the SPAC, who put in $10 each, end up with publicly traded shares of the combined company worth $10 each.

If the SPAC sponsor doesn\xe2\x80\x99t find a deal before, typically, the two-year deadline put on the SPAC, she has to give her public shareholders their $200 million back with interest. If she does find a deal, she has to put it to a vote of the public shareholders; they can vote it down if they don\xe2\x80\x99t think it\xe2\x80\x99s a good deal. Even if they vote in favor, they can redeem their stock: Instead of taking new shares in the combined company, the SPAC shareholders can ask for their $10 back (with interest).

That is the basic story, but it\xc2\xa0is too simplified, and\xc2\xa0in the real world things won\xe2\x80\x99t quite work this way. In the real world the valuation of the private target company will be uncertain, and there will be a negotiation about how much of the combined company the SPAC\xe2\x80\x99s shareholders should get. The SPAC sponsor might reasonably say: \xe2\x80\x9cLook, you are a risky private company, your valuation is uncertain, and I am\xc2\xa0doing you a favor \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0I am providing a service \xe2\x80\x94 by giving you publicly traded stock. You and I both think that you are probably worth $800 million, but I want a discount. Let\xe2\x80\x99s do this deal as though you were worth $600 million, so the SPAC shareholders get 25%\xc2\xa0of the company, instead of 20%.\xe2\x80\x9d If the target company agrees, and if they\xe2\x80\x99re right that the real value is $800 million, then the public investors will end up with 25% of a combined company worth $1 billion. They put in $200 million and got back shares worth $250 million; each of their public shares will be worth $12.50. And in fact at the peak of the 2020-2021 SPAC boom, this happened a lot: SPACs would announce deals with private companies, and their shares would trade up, to $12.50 or higher, because the SPACs were getting a good deal. [1]

Now, things could go the other way. In fact there was eventually a glut of SPACs looking for deals, and the negotiating leverage flipped. The target company\xe2\x80\x99s founder might reasonably say: \xe2\x80\x9cLook, you are a SPAC in a glut of SPACs, you need a deal, and I am\xc2\xa0doing you a favor\xc2\xa0by giving you a target. You and I both think that I am worth $800 million, but I want a premium. Let\xe2\x80\x99s do this deal as though I was worth $1 billion, so your SPAC shareholders get 16.7% of the company instead of 20%.\xe2\x80\x9d Then the SPAC\xe2\x80\x99s public shareholders would get shares of the combined company worth $8.33. [2] Except that if this happened \xe2\x80\x94 if the SPAC agreed to do a deal at a value that everyone knew made the shares worth less than $10 \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0the shareholders would just say no. They would vote to reject the deal, or at least, they\xe2\x80\x99d redeem their shares: Much better to get back $10 with interest than keep shares worth $8.33. SPACs can do deals at a discount (if they can talk their targets into those deals!), but not at a premium.

Of course I am still oversimplifying. For one thing, I am still assuming that everyone basically knows the valuation of the private company: The SPAC and the target might negotiate for a premium or discount, but the \xe2\x80\x9creal\xe2\x80\x9d value is transparent to the market. That\xe2\x80\x99s not really true, and it is not hard to find SPAC deals that traded up when they were announced and eventually traded down as the real value of the company became clearer. In reality it is not at all uncommon for SPAC shareholders to trade their $10 for shares that ultimately turn out to be\xc2\xa0worth\xc2\xa0less than that. When that happens, the shareholders might, in hindsight, feel like they were deceived. The SPAC sponsor announced the deal with great excitement, the shareholders believed it, they kept their shares, thinking that they\xe2\x80\x99d be worth way more than $10, and they turned out to be worth less.

But I have also oversimplified by omitting some important structural elements of SPACs. In fact, when the SPAC sponsor raises $200 million at the very beginning of this process, she doesn\xe2\x80\x99t do it just by selling 20 million shares. She sells 20 million \xe2\x80\x9cunits,\xe2\x80\x9d and each unit consists of (1) one share and (2) some number of warrants , options to buy another share at some higher price. (Generally the warrant exercise price is $11.50, a 15% premium to the initial price of the stock, and each unit comes with a fraction of a warrant, often one-quarter or\xc2\xa0one-third.) After the SPAC goes public, the warrants and shares will trade separately. Ultimately, if the SPAC closes a deal, each share can be redeemed for $10 or roll over into a share of the combined company, but each warrant can only roll over to become a warrant of the combined company. And if the SPAC does not close a deal, each share can be redeemed for $10, but each warrant expires worthless.

Also, when the SPAC sponsor raises $200 million at the beginning of the process, there are not just 20 million SPAC shares outstanding. In fact the SPAC sponsor will generally give herself 20% ownership of the SPAC \xe2\x80\x94 5 million shares \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0for free as a reward for her efforts in setting it up and finding a deal. [3] If she fails to find a deal, those shares are canceled: She has to return $10 per share to the public shareholders, $200 million total, which uses up all of the money in the pot, and there\xe2\x80\x99s nothing left for her. (In fact she will lose money, because she paid the SPAC\xe2\x80\x99s startup costs, advisory fees etc. out of her own pocket.) But if she does find a deal, her 5 million shares roll over into shares of the combined company.

(One more thing that I am omitting is that SPAC deals are traditionally done alongside PIPEs, private investments in public equity, where the sponsor and/or some other big institutional investors put\xc2\xa0more money into the combined company\xc2\xa0at $10 per share. I am going to continue omitting that \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and in fact as the SPAC glut got shakier PIPEs were\xc2\xa0not universal \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but if there is a PIPE it considerably softens some of the criticisms in the rest of this story.)

Now let\xe2\x80\x99s go back to the simple math, but with these more realistic facts. The sponsor finds a private company worth $800 million, and they agree to a merger on exact fair-value terms. The SPAC contributes $200 million, the combined company is worth $1 billion, so the SPAC gets back 20% of the combined company, which is worth $200 million. But now the SPAC has to divide up that value. There are not 20 million shares of the SPAC: There are 25 million, the 20 million public shares and the sponsor\xe2\x80\x99s 5 million free shares. Each of these shares is worth $8. The sponsor gets 5 million shares worth $40 million for free, but that $40 million comes directly out of the public shareholders\xe2\x80\x99 ownership stake. They put in $10 and get back shares worth $8.

Also there are the warrants. In this stylized example, where we know the stock is worth $8 and the warrants are worthless unless the stock gets to $11.50, the warrants aren\xe2\x80\x99t worth much. But in the real world, where stocks are volatile and value is uncertain, the warrants will have some value, and that value will dilute the other holders of the combined company. The target company might say: \xe2\x80\x9cOkay fine, we are worth $800 million, you have $200 million, the combined company is worth $1 billion and we will give you 20% of that value. But that 20% has to be divided among your shares and your warrants; we are not going to give your shareholders 20% of the company and then also let your warrantholders exercise their warrants for another 20%. [4] That would be unfair. We\xe2\x80\x99ll give your shareholders, say, 16% of the combined company\xe2\x80\x99s stock, to reflect the fact that if things go well your warrantholders will get another big chunk of the stock.\xe2\x80\x9d (Don\xe2\x80\x99t worry about the exact number \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0it will depend on, basically, how valuable you think the warrants are, how close they are to being a share of stock \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but the point is that you should attribute some value to the SPAC\xe2\x80\x99s warrants, which will reduce the value that you attribute to its stock.)

These things are problems. They suggest that if a SPAC ever agrees to a merger at exactly fair value \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0where the SPAC puts in $200 million and gets back exactly $200 million worth of the combined company \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0then the SPAC\xe2\x80\x99s public shareholders are getting hosed. Their $10 investment turns into stock worth $8 at most, probably $7 or less. Of course they can vote no on the deal, and/or redeem their stock, and this math suggests that they always should, and that if they vote yes they are somehow confused or deceived.

The simplest\xc2\xa0solution here is for the SPAC to negotiate such a good deal \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and for the combined company to perform so well \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0that the shares are worth $12 anyway. Like, if the SPAC contributes $200 million and gets back $400 million worth of value, that\xe2\x80\x99s plenty to make sure that the sponsor gets her free shares, the warrantholders get their warrants, and the public shareholders get shares worth more than $10. In the peak days of SPAC enthusiasm, that kind of worked, through some combination of:

  • SPACs seemed like an attractive way to go public, so private companies were willing to merge with them at\xc2\xa0discounts;
  • you needed a PIPE deal alongside the SPAC, so if a deal was going to get\xc2\xa0done then target companies had to agree on a valuation that made the common shares worth $10 [5] ;
  • the market was going up generally;
  • SPAC shareholders were particularly enthusiastic, so they kept bidding up de-SPAC mergers; and
  • possibly people did not understand this stuff very clearly?

But over time, as the market stopped rising, SPACs became better understood, and a glut of SPACs pursued a smaller universe of good deals, these things became real problems. The deep problem with a SPAC is that the SPAC\xe2\x80\x99s sponsor takes out 20% of the value of the money that she raises, and the warrantholders get a cut too, and somebody needs to pay them that cut . Either the target company pays it (by giving up more of the company for the SPAC proceeds), or the public SPAC shareholders pay it\xc2\xa0(by getting shares worth $7 for their $10 investment). Somebody is getting a bad deal.

The argument above is loosely based on a well-known\xc2\xa02020 paper called \xe2\x80\x9c A Sober Look at SPACs ,\xe2\x80\x9d by Michael Klausner, Michael Ohlrogge and Emily Ruan. From the abstract:

We find that costs embedded in the SPAC structure are subtle, opaque, higher than has been previously recognized, and higher than the cost of an IPO. Although SPACs raise $10.00 per share from investors in their IPOs, by the time a SPAC merges with a private company to take it public, the SPAC holds far less in net cash per share to contribute to the combined company. For SPACs that merged during our primary sample period of January 2019 through June 2020, mean and median net cash per share were $4.10 and $5.70, respectively. Between June 2020 and November 2021, net cash per share was somewhat higher but far below $10. We find that SPAC costs are not born by the companies they take public, but instead by the SPAC shareholders who hold shares at the time SPACs merge. These investors experience steep post-merger losses, while SPAC sponsors profit handsomely.

Intuitively, if the \xe2\x80\x9cnet cash per share\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0that is, the money\xc2\xa0that the SPAC has in its pot, divided by the real number of shares including free sponsor shares and warrants \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0is $5.70, then either target companies are getting a bad deal (giving up $10 of value for $5.70 of cash), or public SPAC shareholders are getting a bad deal (getting $5.70 of value for $10 of cash). And Klausner et al. find that it\xe2\x80\x99s mostly the shareholders getting the bad deal. Their article \xe2\x80\x9cconcludes by suggesting that the SEC promulgate disclosure requirements specific to SPAC mergers that make clear SPACs\xe2\x80\x99\xc2\xa0costs and sponsors\xe2\x80\x99 incentives.\xe2\x80\x9d

But that\xe2\x80\x99s just an academic paper. Putting his money where his mouth is, though, Klausner went out to sue some SPACs, and last week he won a round :

A court ruling Wednesday bolstered research that argues the true worth of blank-check company mergers is often far below the typical $10-a-share valuation.

The Delaware Chancery Court ruling found that the GigCapital3 Inc. special purpose acquisition company must face claims that public shareholders were denied crucial information, including how much money shares were worth in the purchase of an electric vehicle manufacturer.

The GigCapital3 SPAC shareholders couldn\xe2\x80\x99t make an informed decision on whether to cash out shares or participate in its purchase of Lightning eMotors, court Vice Chancellor Lori Will ruled. \xe2\x80\xa6

Stanford Law Professor Michael Klausner developed a financial model that argues the true value of SPAC shares are much lower than $10-a-share. He brought the court case in August 2021 against GigCapital3 alongside lawyers from Grant & Eisenhofer. The suit was a rare move for a self-described \xe2\x80\x9cobscure academic.\xe2\x80\x9d

In the Gig3 case, the sponsor\xe2\x80\x99s initial $25,000 investment had an implied market value at the time of the merger worth $39 million\xe2\x80\x94a 155,900% return, Klausner argued. Sponsors only earn those returns when a deal is completed.

Will\xe2\x80\x99s ruling accepted Klausner\xe2\x80\x99s calculations. Part of the information GigCapital3 shareholders didn\xe2\x80\x99t receive was that the shares the SPAC used to purchase Lightning eMotors were worth around $5.25 per share\xe2\x80\x94not $10, according to Will.

\xe2\x80\x9cIf Gig3 had less than $6 per share to contribute to the merger, the proxy\xe2\x80\x99s statement that Gig3 shares were worth $10 each was false\xe2\x80\x94or at least materially misleading,\xe2\x80\x9d she wrote. \xe2\x80\x9cMoreover, Gig3 stockholders could not logically expect to receive $10 per share of value in exchange.\xe2\x80\x9d

Here is the opinion . The merger closed in May 2021, when about 71% of Gig3\xe2\x80\x99s public shareholders agreed to exchange their shares for shares in the newly public Lightning instead of redeeming at $10 per share. The stock closed at $0.5161 on Friday. Shareholders who did not redeem \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0who effectively traded $10 for shares worth $0.5161 \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0sued, claiming that Gig3\xe2\x80\x99s sponsors did the deal not because it was good for their\xc2\xa0shareholders (it turned out not to be) but because it got the sponsors $39 million of free stock.\xc2\xa0Gig3/Lightning tried to have the lawsuit dismissed, but the Chancery Court let it go ahead.

There are some bad facts \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0Gig3 tried to do a $100+ million PIPE deal to raise more money and validate the price it was paying, but ultimately raised only $25 million from a big existing Lighting shareholder, so it ended up selling convertible notes at pretty onerous terms; the prospectus for the merger had optimistic financial projections that mostly didn\xe2\x80\x99t work out \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but mostly the problems here are the problems of any SPAC. In any SPAC, the sponsor has incentives to do a deal, any deal, since she gets millions of dollars of free stock if a deal closes and loses the startup costs of the SPAC if it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t. And in any SPAC, the SPAC\xe2\x80\x99s pot of cash is not really worth $10 per share \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0it\xe2\x80\x99s worth $8 per share or less \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0so it is hard for the sponsor to find a deal worth $10 per share or more.

Everything that I wrote above is \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0correct? \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0and \xe2\x80\xa6\xc2\xa0intuitive? \xe2\x80\x9cIf Gig3 had less than $6 per share to contribute to the merger,\xe2\x80\x9d then \xe2\x80\x9cGig3 stockholders could not logically expect to receive $10 per share of value in exchange.\xe2\x80\x9d But that\xe2\x80\x99s just logic. There is some practical sense in which, in like 2020, the answer was pretty much \xe2\x80\x9cehhh there is enough value to go around here, it\xe2\x80\x99s fine, everything goes up, don\xe2\x80\x99t worry about how much the sponsor is getting paid or who\xe2\x80\x99s paying them.\xe2\x80\x9d Now things are tighter, and there is reason to worry.

Elsewhere in conflicts

As part of a broad restructuring , Credit Suisse Group AG has announced that it will spin out its investment banking business into a separate company, which will revive the old First Boston name. New First Boston will be owned partly by Credit Suisse, partly by outside investors\xc2\xa0and partly by its employees. To spin off First Boston, Credit Suisse needed to give it a chief executive officer, and it chose Michael Klein for the job. Klein is a former Citigroup Inc. banker who was also on Credit Suisse\xe2\x80\x99s supervisory board. There is an obvious conflict of interest there: Credit Suisse\xe2\x80\x99s board decided on a strategy that involved handing ownership of its banking business over to the employees of that business, and one member of that board was also appointed to run \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0and, thus, own \xe2\x80\x94 that business. Bloomberg\xe2\x80\x99s Dinesh Nair, Sridhar Natarajan and Eyk Henning note :

The Swiss bank already faced questions when Klein, a member of the board committee that decided to carve out CS First Boston, was appointed to run it. The firm\xe2\x80\x99s chairman has said Klein abstained from some votes on the topic.

Klein stepped down from Credit Suisse\xe2\x80\x99s supervisory board to become CEO designate of CS First Boston as part of the bank\xe2\x80\x99s major strategic revamp in October.

But it\xe2\x80\x99s even more complicated than that because Klein already runs, and owns, an investment bank. It is called M. Klein & Co., so you can hardly expect him to quit. (Actually his brother Mark is also an executive there, so even if Michael Klein left, it could still accurately be called M. Klein & Co.) So the solution is for Credit Suisse to buy Klein\xe2\x80\x99s firm and combine it with First Boston. Nair, Natarajan and Henning:

Credit Suisse Group AG is close to an agreement to buy Michael Klein\xe2\x80\x99s advisory boutique after several rounds of tense negotiations, according to people briefed on the talks.

As part of the expected agreement that\xe2\x80\x99s been hashed out in recent days, the bank will value the firm founded by the incoming head of its First Boston spinout at around a few hundred million dollars, said the people, who asked not to be identified as talks are private. The Swiss firm engaged Deutsche Bank AG to provide a fairness opinion, two of the people said. \xe2\x80\xa6

The deal is expected to allow M. Klein & Co. shareholders to take an eventual stake in First Boston using proceeds from the sale, the people said. Klein and top executives at the Swiss bank clashed in recent weeks over issues including the value of the boutique, with the two sides at one point hundreds of millions of dollars apart on its value, the people said. \xe2\x80\xa6

Mark Klein, Michael\xe2\x80\x99s brother and an executive and stakeholder at M. Klein & Co., is likely to join First Boston as part of the deal, the people said, which could raise further questions about corporate governance.

I\xe2\x80\x99m sure it\xe2\x80\x99s fine, there\xe2\x80\x99s a fairness opinion, and it\xe2\x80\x99s not like I have better ideas. I can see why Credit Suisse wants to retreat from investment banking, why it wants to get some value from this business\xc2\xa0and why it thinks Klein is the best person to do it.\xc2\xa0But \xe2\x80\xa6 what is the good way for this to go? If First Boston ends up being a massive success with a $100 billion valuation, then in hindsight this deal is going to look like \xe2\x80\x9cthe board of Credit Suisse voted to take\xc2\xa0a $100 billion business from shareholders and give it to one board member, plus his brother.\xe2\x80\x9d If First Boston ends up being a failure, it is going to look like \xe2\x80\x9cCredit Suisse took a perfectly good business and handed it over to one of its board members, plus his brother, who broke it.\xe2\x80\x9d If you are a high-powered investment banker you are in some\xc2\xa0sense in the business of managing conflicts of interest \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0your job is to be friends with everybody who matters, and any time you represent a seller you will be thinking a little bit about how you can win future business from the buyer \xe2\x80\x94\xc2\xa0but this one seems particularly tough.

Everything is securities fraud

If the chief executive officer of a public company exercises poor judgment and engages in an inappropriate personal relationship with an employee, is that securities fraud? You know my rule: Every bad thing that a public company does is also securities fraud, this is a bad thing, therefore this is securities fraud. Here\xe2\x80\x99s the\xc2\xa0US Securities and Exchange Commission enforcement action :

The Securities and Exchange Commission today charged Stephen J. Easterbrook, former CEO of McDonald\xe2\x80\x99s Corporation, with making false and misleading statements to investors about the circumstances leading to his termination in November 2019. McDonald\xe2\x80\x99s also was charged for shortcomings in its public disclosures related to Easterbrook\xe2\x80\x99s separation agreement.

According to the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s order, McDonald\xe2\x80\x99s terminated Easterbrook for exercising poor judgment and engaging in an inappropriate personal relationship with a McDonald\xe2\x80\x99s employee in violation of company policy. However, McDonald\xe2\x80\x99s and Easterbrook entered into a separation agreement that concluded his termination was without cause, which allowed him to retain substantial equity compensation that otherwise would have been forfeited. In making this conclusion, McDonald\xe2\x80\x99s exercised discretion that was not disclosed to investors.

Subsequently, in July 2020, McDonald\xe2\x80\x99s discovered through an internal investigation that Easterbrook had engaged in other undisclosed, improper relationships with additional McDonald\xe2\x80\x99s employees. According to the SEC\xe2\x80\x99s order, Easterbrook knew or was reckless in not knowing that his failure to disclose these additional violations of company policy prior to his termination would influence McDonald\xe2\x80\x99s disclosures to investors related to his departure and compensation.

"When corporate officers corrupt internal processes to manage their personal reputations or line their own pockets, they breach their fundamental duties to shareholders, who are entitled to transparency and fair dealing from executives," said Gurbir S. Grewal, Director of the Division of Enforcement. "By allegedly concealing the extent of his misconduct during the company\xe2\x80\x99s internal investigation, Easterbrook broke that trust with \xe2\x80\x93 and ultimately misled \xe2\x80\x93 shareholders."

The general form of \xe2\x80\x9ceverything is securities fraud\xe2\x80\x9d is:

  • The company, or one of its executives, does a bad thing;
  • The company does not simultaneously disclose the bad thing\xc2\xa0to shareholders (because it is shameful, etc.);
  • Eventually the bad thing comes out and the stock drops;
  • The shareholders can say they were defrauded: They paid more for the stock than they would have if they had known about the bad thing.

When I write about this, sometimes I get complaints from securities lawyers who say \xe2\x80\x9cno, securities fraud doesn\xe2\x80\x99t work that way: You are not required to disclose everything immediately , and it is only securities fraud if the company puts out some misleading disclosure. Just not mentioning a bad thing is not fraud.\xe2\x80\x9d [6] And so in fact the technical game of \xe2\x80\x9ceverything is securities fraud\xe2\x80\x9d is that you have to find something that the company did say that turns out not to be true in light of the bad thing. A classic that we have discussed a few times is that, if a company has a public code of ethics saying \xe2\x80\x9cour executives are not allowed to do bad stuff,\xe2\x80\x9d and they do bad stuff, then you sue the company because its code of ethics is misleading. Or if the company has a risk factor that says \xe2\x80\x9cbad stuff might happen and that would be bad,\xe2\x80\x9d and the bad stuff has already happened, then that risk factor is misleading.

Or, here, if the company fires an executive for having an intra-office affair, and it puts out a press release saying that he \xe2\x80\x9cseparated from the Company following the Board\xe2\x80\x99s determination that he violated company policy and demonstrated poor judgment involving a recent consensual relationship with an employee,\xe2\x80\x9d but it doesn\xe2\x80\x99t say that he had several consensual relationships with employees, well, I guess that use of the singular is misleading?

Sorry JPMorgan!

At FT Alphaville, Bryce Elder reports that JPMorgan Chase & Co.\xc2\xa0 is building a computer program to tell you what\xe2\x80\x99s in the news, \xe2\x80\x9cto help investors with their allocation decisions by tracking the evolution of themes over time.\xe2\x80\x9d Apparently their previous efforts failed because of, um, me:

This is JPMorgan\xe2\x80\x99s second attempt at getting a computer to read the news. Its first go struggled because \xe2\x80\x9csome of our detected themes were being driven by only one or two sources\xe2\x80\x9d, it said. \xe2\x80\x9cThe problem is exacerbated if a source publishes its entire output on just one theme at great volume.\xe2\x80\x9d (Cf, its first dataset ran to November 15 and Bloomberg Businessweek published The Crypto Story on October 25.)

Oops! Sorry. I hope you did not use this signal to get extremely long crypto on Oct. 25. I periodically remind my readers that nothing in this column is ever legal or investing advice, but I should emphasize that anything that your computer algorithm manages to pull out of this column is particularly not legal or investing advice. Sometimes it\xe2\x80\x99s jokes! What is your computer gonna do with that?

Things happen

Goldman to Cut About 3,200 Jobs This Week After Cost Review. HSBC Sends Recruitment Emails to Rival Bankers Facing Cuts. The Murdoch family trust : how the scions could battle for control. Jack Ma Cedes Control of Fintech Giant Ant Group. Crypto Empire DCG Faces US Investigation Over Internal Transfers. Celsius founder Mashinsky sued for fraud by New York attorney-general. Why Gemini Kept Pushing Risky Accounts as Crypto Market Tumbled. FTX Reaches Bankruptcy Cooperation Deal With Bahamas Liquidators. FTX Seeks to Recoup Sam Bankman-Fried\xe2\x80\x99s Charitable Donations. \xe2\x80\x98Spectacular\xe2\x80\x99 Trading Drop Plagues Still-Reeling Crypto Market. Brother of Criminal Bitcoin Mixing CEO Pleads Guilty to Stealing 712 Bitcoins From IRS . Companies Face Rising Pressure to Offer ESG Retirement Options . Deere to Allow Farmers to Repair Their Own Equipment . Silicon Valley horse therapy .\xc2\xa0Silicon Valley Can\xe2\x80\x99t Quit Its Pizza Robot Obsession .

If you'd like to get\xc2\xa0Money\xc2\xa0Stuff\xc2\xa0in handy email form, right in your inbox, please subscribe at this link . Or you can subscribe to Money Stuff and other great Bloomberg newsletters here . Thanks!

[1] Or at least, because their investors thought they were. Often these deals ended up trading poorly in the long run.

[2] That is, the combined company is really worth $1 billion, they get 16.7%, so their $200 million investment turns into $167 million of stock, or $8.33 for each $10 share.

[3] Generally she pays some startup costs and might buy these shares for a nominal amount, but \xe2\x80\x9cfor free\xe2\x80\x9d is the right intuition.

[4] The math doesn\xe2\x80\x99t quite work that way \xe2\x80\x94 warrant exercise increases both the numerator and the denominator \xe2\x80\x94 but you know what I mean.

[5] Also the PIPE would generally be just common equity at $10 per share, which reduced the dilutive impact of the SPAC structural stuff. If you raised $200 million from a SPAC and $300 million from a PIPE, the sponsor\xe2\x80\x99s 5 million free SPAC shares represent only 10% of the total value instead of 25%.

[6] The actual rule , in the US, is that it is fraud, \xe2\x80\x9cin connection with the purchase or sale of any security,\xe2\x80\x9d \xe2\x80\x9cto make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading.\xe2\x80\x9d
